title: A Six Sensor Array of Coated-Wire Electrodes for Use in a Portable Flow Injection Analyzer
abstract:
A Six Sensor Array of Coated-Wire Electrodes for Use in a Portable
Flow Injection Analyzer
Peter W. Alexander,*þ Telis Dimitrakopoulos,þþ and D. Brynn Hibbertþþþ
þ
School of Applied Science, University of Tasmania, P.O. Box 1214, Launceston, Tasmania, 7250, Australia
þþ Present address: Institut fu¨r Instrumentelle Analytik, Forschungszentrum Karlsruhe GmbH, Postfach 3640, D-76021, Karlsruhe, Germany
þþþDepartment of Analytical Chemistry, University of New South Wales, Sydney, 2052, Australia
Received: October 9, 1987
Final version: April 20, 1998
Abstract
An array of ﬁve photo-cured epoxydiacrylate membrane based coated-wire electrodes and a Ag/AgCl wire electrode used as a chloride sensor were
all used simultaneously in a previously reported multielectrode ﬂow cell and in a portable ﬂow injection analyzer [1]. Photo-cured coated-wire
electrodes for ammonium, calcium, hydrogen (pH), nitrate and potassium and Ag/AgCl wire based chloride electrode were developed for remote-
site monitoring. The photo-cured membranes prepared exhibited strong adhesion to the metal substrate and showed improved mechanical strength
compared to poly(vinyl chloride) based membranes. Each sensor in the present electrode-array exhibited near-Nernstian response over a log-linear
range of between 0.1 mM and 10 mM with detection limits of 0.01 mM in the ﬂow injection potentiometric mode. The ﬂow injection cell was
capable of determining the respective ions in various water samples and the results were in good agreement with comparative analytical methods.
Keywords: Portable instrumentation, Flow injection potentiometry, Photo-cured liquid polymer membranes, Coated-wire electrode array
1. Introduction
There has been considerable interest in developing multielec-
trode potentiometric
systems for
the
simultaneous
multiion
determination of aqueous samples in either ﬂow-through [1–10]
or steady-state measurements [11, 12]. Ion-selective electrodes
(ISEs)
in
ﬂow
injection
potentiometry
(FIP)
have
several
advantages compared to steady-state measurements. They include,
fast sample throughput (>120 injections/hour), high precision, small
sample volumes (<300 mL), economical use of reagents, correction
of electrode drift by the measurement of peak heights and ease of
computer automation [13, 14]. The need for ﬁeld-portable
instrumentation based on ﬂow injection analysis (FIA) has been
reviewed recently [15], and the feasibility of employing commer-
cial ISEs in a portable battery-powered ﬂow injection analyzer [16],
as well as an example of multisensor array derived from three
commercial planar type ISEs [17] have been demonstrated.
As demonstrated in previous studies, photo-cured epoxydiacry-
late membrane ISEs have some advantages over the commonly
used poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) membrane based ISEs [18–29].
Some advantages of photo-cured membrane ISEs compared to PVC
based ISEs include an improved adhesion on metal substrates,
greater mechanical strength and rapid manufacture (<40 min) [19].
In addition, photo-cured epoxydiacrylate membranes generally are
fast responding and exhibit excellent selectivity, making them
ideally suited for ﬂow-through systems. Photo-cured sensors
developed to date include ammonium [29], calcium [19–22],
potassium [23, 24], pH [25, 29], nitrate [26], sodium [27] and
lithium [28], most of which have been used in the FIP mode. Other
polymer materials that have been photo-cured for potentiometric
sensors include poly(methyl methacrylate) [18], polyurethane [30]
and poly (decyl methacrylate) [31].
Previous work in the area of multiion potentiometric array
systems have involved both ISEs [6–10] and ion-selective ﬁeld
effect transistors (ISFETs) [2, 3, 5] in ﬂow-through arrangement.
There has been numerous multisensor array systems that employ
chemometric methods for the optimization of individual sensors in
an array, in terms of the selectivity and the electrode slope [6–9, 11].
Statistical methods employed include partial least squares [9], non-
linear regression [11], simplex optimization [6, 7] and projection
pursuit regression [8].
In controlled laboratory experiments the above arrays have given
excellent results. However, it has not been demonstrated that the
chemometric methods are sufﬁciently robust to be useful in the
ﬁeld. In principle, however, anyone of these methods could be
implemented on a portable notebook computer, and future systems
may include on-line sophisticated data analysis. Examples of
multisensor array systems for use in FIP that do not require
chemometric corrections have been reported for an array of six
commercial ISEs [10] and an array of four CWEs [3, 4].
This article reports the development of an array consisting of ﬁve
photo-cured epoxydiacrylate membrane electrodes incorporating
ionophores
for
ammonium,
calcium,
hydrogen,
nitrate
and
potassium and a Ag/AgCl wire electrode for chloride. The electrode
array is incorporated in a multielectrode ﬂow cell in a portable ﬂow
injection potentiometric analyzer which is designed for remote site
monitoring [1]. The system was used to determine the six ions
simultaneously in hydroponic nutrient solutions and wastewater
samples from a local greenhouse grower as well as a bottled mineral
water sample.
2. Experimental
All reagents were used as received from the suppliers:
ammonium nitrate, calcium chloride, potassium nitrate and lithium
acetate (all BDH, AnalaR). Stock solutions ranging between
0.01 mM and 100 mM of ammonium nitrate, calcium chloride and
potassium nitrate were each prepared by serial dilution. A universal
pH buffer stock solution containing 10 mM sodium dihydrogen
phosphate, 2.5 mM sodium tetraborate and 7 mM trisodium citrate
(all BDH, AnalaR) was prepared. The mixed ammonium nitrate/
calcium chloride/potassium nitrate/pH buffer standard solutions
were prepared daily by diluting the appropriate stock solution 10
fold and the pH adjusted with either 50 mM H2SO4 or 100 mM
NaOH. An Activon pH glass combination electrode (Model
AEP412) and pH/mV meter (Model AS209) was used to determine
the pH of the prepared ammonium nitrate/calcium chloride/
potassium nitrate/pH buffer solutions. Ultrapure water (Barnstead
Ultrapure water systems, 18.0 MQ) was used throughout this study.
The polymer matrix of the photo-cured membrane prepared in
this study consisted of Ebecryl 600 (bisphenol A epoxydiacrylate)
707
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 10
q WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
1040-0397/98/1008-0707 $ 17.50þ.50/0
 and Uvecryl P36 (copolymerisable benzophenone photoinitiator),
both of which were obtained from UCB Chemical Sector
(Belgium), and 1,6-hexanediol diacrylate was obtained from
Anchor Chemicals Australia Pty. Ltd. The initiator used in the
photo-cured ammonium, nitrate and potassium membranes was a
mixture of 9,10-phenanthrenequinone and diphenyliodonium chlor-
ide, both obtained from Fluka (Switzerland). The lipophilic additives
used were potassium
tetrakis(4-chlorophenyl)borate (KClTPB)
and ammonium tetrakis(4-chlorophenyl)borate (TAClTPB), both
obtained from Fluka. The ionophores used were nonactin for
ammonium, calcium bis[4-(10,10,30,30-tetramethylbutyl) phenyl]-
phosphate (CaTBPP) for calcium, N,N-dioctadecylmethylamine
for hydrogen, 2,20-bis[3,4-(15-crown-5-)-2-nitrophenylcarbamoxy-
methyl]tetradecane (BME-44) for potassium and were obtained
from Fluka. The nitrate (TDA-NO3) ionophore was prepared from
tetradodecylammonium bromide (Fluka), and converted to the
nitrate salt by a solvent extraction procedure. The plasticizers used
were dioctyl phenylphosphonate (DOPP), obtained from Sigma
Chemical Co, bis(2-ethylhexyl)phthalate (BEHP) and o-nitrophenyl
octyl ether (NPOE) were both obtained from Fluka.
2.1. Preparation of the Coated-Wire Electrodes
The chloride CWE was prepared by electrochemically anodizing
a silver wire (0.8 mm id) in 100 mM potassium chloride [32]. The
precured membrane compositions of the ammonium, calcium,
hydrogen, potassium and nitrate CWEs are presented in Table 1.
Precured membranes were ultrasonically mixed for 3 h and then
using thin nylon brushes were each applied directly onto 0.8 mm
diameter silver wires embedded in a previously reported ﬂow cell
[1].
The
membranes
were
cured
using UV lamp
(250 W,
l ¼ 300 nm) under a nitrogen atmosphere for up to 5 min. The
membranes produced were hard and transparent. The resultant photo-
cured membrane CWEs were washed with a 10 % (v/v) ethanol
solution and nanopure water for 5 min and then conditioned
overnight in a 10mM calcium chloride/potassium nitrate/ammonium
nitrate (pH 5.00) solution.
2.2 Design of the Multielectrode Flow Cell
An eight-sensor ﬂow cell used in a portable battery-powered ﬂow
injection analyzer that was previously reported [1] was used in this
study, with six of the available electrode positions occupied by
CWEs. The CWEs were positioned in one channel, while the silver/
silver chloride reference electrode was located in a separate channel
and these two channels were merged downstream. The design of the
analog-to-digital converter (ADC) employed in the portable ﬂow
injection system allows for liquid polymer membranes of up to 100
MQ resistance to be used as shown in previous work [1].
2.3. Potentiometric Measurement Procedures
The portable FIP system described previously [1] was used at
ﬂow rates between 1.5 and 2.0 mL/min through 2 channels, one for
the reference electrode and the other for the six-CWE array. The
ammonium nitrate/calcium chloride/potassium nitrate/pH buffer
standard solutions were introduced manually using a modiﬁed
Rheodyne (5020) injection valve ﬁtted with a 200 mL sample
volume. The carrier and reference streams used for all ﬂow
injection measurements were 3 mM lithium acetate containing
0.005 mM ammonium nitrate, calcium chloride and potassium
nitrate at pH between 6.9 and 6.5.
Steady-state measurements were performed by continuously
pumping each standard solution into the ﬂow channel containing
the photo-cured electrodes. The reference solution used was 10 mM
potassium chloride with all steady-state measurements. The
selectivity coefﬁcients for the ammonium, calcium, chloride,
nitrate and potassium CWEs were determined using the matched
potential method [33], while the hydrogen CWE was determined
using the ﬁxed interference method [34].
The data acquired for each of the CWEs were displayed
simultaneously in real time millivolt readings on a single computer
window using a data acquisition software which was written in
Think C. Data analysis and graphical representation of the data with
standard software packages allowed calculation of the electrode
slope, the E0 and the standard deviations (at the 95 % conﬁdence
level) of a given CWE in either steady-state or FIP modes.
2.4. Comparative Analytical Methods
A Varian SpectrAA-300 atomic absorption spectrometer was
used to analyze samples for potassium. The calcium FIP results
were compared to a commercial calcium ISE (Model ASE502)
obtained from Activon (Sydney, Australia) that was used in a wall-
jet type ﬂow cell [35]. A Pye Unicam SP6-300 UV/vis spectro-
photometer equipped with a ﬂow-through cuvette suitable for ﬂow
injection was used to determine ammonium as the salicylic acid
analog of indophenol blue [36], while the chloride FIP results were
compared to a UV/vis spectrophotometric ﬂow injection method
as described previously [13]. Nitrate was determined by ion
chromatography using a Millipore Waters IC-Pak A column
(50 mm × 4.6 mm, 10 mm) and conductivity detector (Model 430).

This article reports the development of an array consisting of ﬁve
photo-cured epoxydiacrylate membrane electrodes incorporating
potassium and a Ag/AgCl wire electrode for chloride. The electrode
array is incorporated in a multielectrode ﬂow cell in a portable ﬂow
injection potentiometric analyzer which is designed for remote site
monitoring [1]. The system was used to determine the six ions
simultaneously in hydroponic nutrient solutions and wastewater
samples from a local greenhouse grower as well as a bottled mineral

ammonium nitrate, calcium chloride, potassium nitrate and lithium
acetate (all BDH, AnalaR). Stock solutions ranging between

potassium nitrate were each prepared by serial dilution. A universal
pH buffer stock solution containing 10 mM sodium dihydrogen
phosphate, 2.5 mM sodium tetraborate and 7 mM trisodium citrate
(all BDH, AnalaR) was prepared. The mixed ammonium nitrate/
calcium chloride/potassium nitrate/pH buffer standard solutions
were prepared daily by diluting the appropriate stock solution 10
fold and the pH adjusted with either 50 mM H2SO4 or 100 mM
NaOH. An Activon pH glass combination electrode (Model

potassium nitrate/pH buffer solutions. Ultrapure water (Barnstead
Ultrapure water systems, 18.0 MQ) was used throughout this study.

2.1. Preparation of the Coated-Wire Electrodes
The chloride CWE was prepared by electrochemically anodizing
a silver wire (0.8 mm id) in 100 mM potassium chloride [32]. The
precured membrane compositions of the ammonium, calcium,
hydrogen, potassium and nitrate CWEs are presented in Table 1.
Precured membranes were ultrasonically mixed for 3 h and then
using thin nylon brushes were each applied directly onto 0.8 mm
diameter silver wires embedded in a previously reported ﬂow cell
l ¼ 300 nm) under a nitrogen atmosphere for up to 5 min. The
membranes produced were hard and transparent. The resultant photo-

solution and nanopure water for 5 min and then conditioned
overnight in a 10mM calcium chloride/potassium nitrate/ammonium
2.2 Design of the Multielectrode Flow Cell
An eight-sensor ﬂow cell used in a portable battery-powered ﬂow
injection analyzer that was previously reported [1] was used in this
study, with six of the available electrode positions occupied by
CWEs. The CWEs were positioned in one channel, while the silver/
silver chloride reference electrode was located in a separate channel
and these two channels were merged downstream. The design of the
analog-to-digital converter (ADC) employed in the portable ﬂow
injection system allows for liquid polymer membranes of up to 100
MQ resistance to be used as shown in previous work [1].
2.3. Potentiometric Measurement Procedures
The portable FIP system described previously [1] was used at
ﬂow rates between 1.5 and 2.0 mL/min through 2 channels, one for
the reference electrode and the other for the six-CWE array. The
ammonium nitrate/calcium chloride/potassium nitrate/pH buffer
standard solutions were introduced manually using a modiﬁed
Rheodyne (5020) injection valve ﬁtted with a 200 mL sample
volume. The carrier and reference streams used for all ﬂow
injection measurements were 3 mM lithium acetate containing
0.005 mM ammonium nitrate, calcium chloride and potassium
nitrate at pH between 6.9 and 6.5.
Steady-state measurements were performed by continuously
pumping each standard solution into the ﬂow channel containing
the photo-cured electrodes. The reference solution used was 10 mM
potassium chloride with all steady-state measurements. The
selectivity coefﬁcients for the ammonium, calcium, chloride,
nitrate and potassium CWEs were determined using the matched
potential method [33], while the hydrogen CWE was determined
using the ﬁxed interference method [34].
The data acquired for each of the CWEs were displayed
simultaneously in real time millivolt readings on a single computer
window using a data acquisition software which was written in
Think C. Data analysis and graphical representation of the data with
standard software packages allowed calculation of the electrode
slope, the E0 and the standard deviations (at the 95 % conﬁdence
level) of a given CWE in either steady-state or FIP modes.
2.4. Comparative Analytical Methods
A Varian SpectrAA-300 atomic absorption spectrometer was
used to analyze samples for potassium. The calcium FIP results
were compared to a commercial calcium ISE (Model ASE502)
obtained from Activon (Sydney, Australia) that was used in a wall-
jet type ﬂow cell [35]. A Pye Unicam SP6-300 UV/vis spectro-
photometer equipped with a ﬂow-through cuvette suitable for ﬂow
injection was used to determine ammonium as the salicylic acid
analog of indophenol blue [36], while the chloride FIP results were
compared to a UV/vis spectrophotometric ﬂow injection method
as described previously [13]. Nitrate was determined by ion
chromatography using a Millipore Waters IC-Pak A column
(50 mm × 4.6 mm, 10 mm) and conductivity detector (Model 430).
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 10
Table 1. Compositions of the photo-cured membrane compositions used in the CWE array.
* Refer to experimental section for the speciﬁc ionophore employed for each CWE membrane.
3.1. Response of Multi-CWE Array in the Steady-State Mode
Figure 1 presents the calibration plots for the photo-cured
ammonium, calcium, chloride, pH, nitrate and potassium CWE
array determined individually in the steady-state mode. The photo-
cured ammonium, nitrate and potassium CWEs all exhibited
Nernstian responses with the log-linear range between 0.01 mM
and 10 mM using pure metal nitrate solutions in the steady-state
mode. The detection limit observed for the ammonium, nitrate and
potassium CWEs were each 0.001 mM for their respective
The photo-cured calcium CWE exhibited an electrode response
of 32.8 6 0.7 mV change per activity decade and a log-linear range
between 0.01 mM and 100 mM, while the chloride CWE exhibited a
near-Nernstian response of ¹55.4 6 0.6 mV change per activity
decade over a log-linear range 0.02 mM and 200 mM using pure
calcium chloride solutions, in the steady-state mode. Detection
limits for the calcium and chloride CWEs were 0.001 mM calcium
chloride for their respective determinand. The pH CWE exhibited a
near-Nernstian response of 56.3 6 0.4 mV change/pH unit over a
pH range between 4.0 and 11.0 in the steady-state mode. Response
times observed for each of the to attain 90 % of the steady-state
response were typically between 3 s and 5 s, making all these
sensors ideally suited for FIP measurements. Typical electrode
drifts observed for these sensors were < 5 mV/day for a period of
weeks, during which time the electrode coated remained transparent
The selectivity coefﬁcients for the photo-cured ammonium,
calcium, chloride, nitrate, pH and potassium CWEs are presented in
Table 2. The ammonium, calcium, pH and potassium CWEs
exhibit excellent selectivity against common interfering ions as
described in previous studies [20, 21, 24, 29]. The chloride CWE
exhibited a negligible interference against most common interfering
ions, as shown in Table 2, and the nitrate CWE exhibited adequate
3.2. Carrier Requirements for Multi-Ion FIP Measurements
The composition of the carrier solution was optimized to provide
the best compromize of performance for each CWE in the ﬂow-
through system. The most difﬁcult sensor to be considered in the
array was the pH electrode used in FIP mode. Recent studies have
been dedicated to deﬁne the crucial parameters required to achieve
reliable potentiometric measurements of pH in a ﬂow-through
system [13, 37] and this deserves further discussion.
To determine pH reliably in FIP, the buffer capacity of the
injected sample zone must be at least 10 times higher than the
carrier [13], otherwise there will be mixing with a consequent
change in the pH of the injected sample zone, resulting in an
(150 mL to 300 mL) in the FIP will also minimize the effect of
mixing the injected sample and the carrier stream [13, 37]. It is also
important to minimize the pH difference between the injected
sample zone and the carrier in order to attain reliable pH
measurements in the FIP mode. Therefore, to measure pH
accurately in the FIP mode the carrier must be close to pH 7.0 in
order to minimize the dispersion of the injected sample zone.
In this study, it was determined that the ideal carrier solution was
3 mM lithium acetate between pH 6.9 and pH 6.5, containing
0.005 mM ammonium nitrate, calcium chloride and potassium
nitrate. The separate reference solution employed was identical to
the carrier solution, to minimize the liquid junction potential at the
conﬂuence point of the multisensor ﬂow cell due to rapid
hydrodynamic mixing [38]. It has been demonstrated that the
presence of a small amount of the determinand (<0.01 mM) in the
carrier solution will automatically condition a given electrode and
improvements in the responsiveness, baseline stability and a greater
sample throughput rate have been achieved [14]. For this reason, the
A Six Sensor Array of Coated-Wire Electrodes
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 10
Fig. 1. Calibration plots determined individually for photo-cured ammo-
nium, calcium, pH, nitrate and potassium CWEs and a Ag/AgCl CWE in the
Table 2. Selectivity coefﬁcients determined for each CWE (i = the determinand).
carrier solution contained 0.005 mM of the determinand for each
CWE used in the multisensor ﬂow-through cell.
The pH proﬁles of each of the photo-cured CWEs exhibited in a
mixed 10 mM calcium chloride-potassium nitrate solution are
presented in Figure 2. No ammonium ion was present in the
solution since the response in the alkaline region (pH > 8.5) would
be dominated by the equilibrium shift to ammonia and would not be
a true representation of the pH independent range of the ammonium
CWE. A universal pH independent range of between 4.5 and 10.0
can be employed for each of the ammonium, calcium, chloride,
nitrate and potassium CWEs. However, the pH range of the mixed
standard solutions containing ammonium and potassium nitrate and
calcium chloride needs to be restricted to between pH 4.5 and 8.5
for two main reasons: 1) standard solutions with < pH 4.5 will
result in an erroneous result for the calcium CWE and 2) the mixed
standards solutions need to be < pH 8.5 in order to avoid the
equilibrium shift from the ammonium ion to ammonia due to the
increasing hydroxide ion level.
3.3. Simultaneous Response of the Multi-CWE Array in FIP
Typical FIP response observed for the six-CWE array simulta-
neously in the FIP mode are presented in Figure 3. In the FIP mode,
the ammonium and potassium CWEs each exhibited an electrode
response of 49.26 1.0 mV and 48.7 6 0.6 mV change per activity
decade, respectively and a log-linear range between 0.01mM and
10mM in the FIP mode. The chloride and the nitrate CWEs exhibited
electrode responses of ¹46.5 6 0.8 mV and ¹49.5 6 0.7 mV change
per activity decade, respectively and a log-linear range between
0.02mMand20 mMinthe FIPmode.Theslope ofthephoto-curedpH
CWE was 49.96 0.6 mV change / pH unit over a pH range between
pH 8.2 and pH 5.1 in the FIP mode. The calcium CWE exhibited a
near-Nernstian slope of 25.8 6 0.4 mV per activity decade over a log-
linear range between 0.01mM and 10 mM in the FIP mode. Figure 4
presentsthe calibration plotsforthemulti-CWE array inthe FIPmode.
The lower than Nernstian response observed for the ammonium,
calcium, chloride, pH, nitrate and potassium CWEs in FIP was
typical of other photo-cured sensors [20, 21, 24, 29] and was due to
the initial slow response time of the CWEs and the dispersion of the
injected sample zone. However, the FIP response observed with the
multi-CWE array represents at least 80 % of the steady-state
response and provides with adequate analytical sensitivity. The FIP
response presented in Figure 3 demonstrates baseline stability and
sample throughput rate of up to 180 injections/h, for each CWE
depending on the concentration injected.
Validation of the six-CWE array in the portable battery-powered
ﬂow injection analyzer was performed using various water samples
that included hydroponic nutrient solutions and wastewater samples
obtained from a local greenhouse grower and a bottled spring
mineral water sample. The FIP results are presented in Table 3. The
FIP results observed for the calcium, pH, potassium and nitrate

injection volume: 200 mL and the total ﬂow rate: 1.6 mL/min and carrier was 3 mM lithium acetate (pH 6.79) containing 0.0005 mM NH4NO3-KNO3-CaCl2;

CWEs consistently exhibited relative errors of less than 5.0 %
compared to the comparative analytical methods employed in this
study, such as atomic absorption spectrometry, ion chromatography
However, the average relative error observed for the FIP
ammonium and chloride results were between 9.1 % and 7.7 %,
respectively, compared to the two UV/vis spectrophotometric
validation methods. This was reasonable with respect to mono-
valent CWEs which are known to have an error of the order of 4.0 %
due to log response of the electrode. In the case of the ammonium
CWE, the likely source of interference could have resulted from the
potassium ions that was up to 11 times more abundant than
ammonium in the hydroponic and wastewater samples. However,
considering the observed selectivity coefﬁcient for the ammonium
CWE against potassium ions, the presence of potassium ions in the
water samples would have no signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the response
In the case of the silver/silver chloride wire electrode used as a
chloride sensor, the selectivity coefﬁcients exhibited against the
main competing anions were excellent as shown in Table 2.
Therefore, it was not possible that the observed FIP results for
chloride were inﬂuenced by the presence of other anions, such as
nitrate, in the water samples used to validate the CWE-array
system. The lower photometric results for the hydroponic and
wastewater samples are likely due to the interference of the high
ferric ion present in the hydroponic and wastewater samples.
The electronic design of the portable battery-powered ﬂow
injection analyzer allows for multiliquid polymer membrane based

CWEs of up to 100 MQ resistance to be used simultaneously in

used in a portable battery-powered ﬂow injection analyzer
described previously [1]. The main advantages of photo-cured
strength, improved adhesion to metal substrates and faster
manufacturing time (< 5 min) compared to the traditional PVC
membranes (2 days), and photo-cured membranes are more suitable
The photo-cured six-CWE array was used in a portable battery-
powered ﬂow injection analyzer to simultaneously determine
ammonium, calcium, chloride, nitrate, pH and potassium ions in
various water samples. The ideal carrier was 3 mM lithium acetate
containing 0.005 mM ammonium nitrate, potassium nitrate and
calcium chloride between pH 6.9 and pH 6.5. The FIP results were
in good agreement with comparative analytical methods.
Future work will involve the development and addition of other
photo-cured membrane based CWEs to the six CWEs presented in
this study. As an automated portable battery-powered FIP system,
the development and trial of software to process multisensor data in
real time with very little involvement by the operator is in progress
and ultimately the development of a ‘black box’ that can be moved
from site to site in the ﬁeld. In the case where the selectivity of
future photo-cured based membranes were inferior, then chemo-
metric methods would have to be employed to correct for a given
